title-group.article-title: Increased Ras GTPase activity is regulated by miRNAs that can be attenuated by CDF treatment in pancreatic cancer cells

article-meta.aff: 1Department of Oncology, Wayne State University School of medicine, Detroit, Michigan
2Pathology, Karmanos Cancer Institute, Wayne State University School of medicine, Detroit, Michigan
3Interdisciplinary Science & Technology Research Academy, Abeda Inamdar Senior College, Azam Campus, Pune 411 001, India

author-notes.corresp: Address for Correspondence: Fazlul H. Sarkar Ph.D. Department of Pathology, Karmanos Cancer Institute Wayne State University School of Medicine 740 Hudson Webber Cancer Research Center, 4100 John R Street Detroit, MI 48201 Tel: 313-576-8327 Fax: 313-576-8389 fsarkar@med.wayne.edu

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2012 Elsevier Ireland Ltd. All rights reserved

license.license-p: This file is available for text mining. It may also be used consistent with the principles of fair use under the copyright law.

related-article.article-title: Retraction notice to “Increased Ras GTPase activity is regulated by miRNAs that can be attenuated by CDF treatment in pancreatic cancer cells” [Cancer Lett. 319(2) (2012) 173–181]

abstract.p: Ras gene is frequently mutated, and also associated with increased Ras expression and its GTPase activity (activity) in pancreatic cancer (PC), which could in part be due to deregulated expression of microRNAs (miRNAs) contributing to tumor aggressiveness. Here we report, for the first time, that Ras expression and its activity were significantly higher in MIAPaCa-2 cells compared to COLO-357 and BxPC-3 cell lines, which was correlated with loss of let-7 family and miR-143 expression in MIAPaCa-2 cells compared to COLO-357 and BxPC-3 cells. Whereas the expression of miR-21, a frequently up-regulated miRNA in solid tumors was up-regulated in MIAPaCa-2 cells and it was correlated with increased Ras expression and its activity. The miRNAs, let-7i and miR-143 was found to target Ras, and forced re-expression of let-7i and miR-143 inhibited Ras activity, cell proliferation and colony formation in vitro. We also found that the treatment of cells in vitro or treatment of MIAPaCa-2 induced tumors in vivo with CDF, a novel synthetic analog of curcumin, led to the re-expression of let-7 and miR-143, and down-regulated miR-21 expression, which was consistent with attenuation of Ras expression and its activity. Moreover, re-expression of let-7iin vivo resulted in decreased tumor growth and Ras activity. These results suggest that the loss of expression of let-7 and miR-143, and increased expression of miR-21 leads to increased expression of Ras and its GTPase activity, which could be attenuated by CDF treatment and, thus CDF could become a novel therapeutic agent for the treatment of PC.

sec.title: 1. Introduction
sec.p: K-Ras is one of the most frequently mutated isoform of the Ras oncogene families that includes H-Ras and N-Ras that are involved in cellular signal transduction, and thus their activation is associated with increased cell growth and differentiation in many cancers including pancreatic cancer (PC) [1]. K-Ras mutation occurs often in colorectal, lung, multiple myeloma, and chronic pancreatitis patients, which subsequently leads to the development of PC [1;2]. Thus, K-Ras plays a key role in the development and maintenance of the malignant phenotype, and the most common mutation was found to be on codon 12 (K-RasG12D), which is related to the activation of Ras GTPase activity [3]. In addition to the activation of Ras due to mutation, the loss or the gain of expression of microRNAs (miRNAs) could also mediate deregulation in the expression of target genes that are important in human cancer [4;5]. Emerging evidence suggest that a number of miRNAs could function as either oncogenes or tumor suppressor [6;7], and the loss of one such miRNA is miR-143 which function as a tumor suppressor in gastric cancer, and further showing that the re-expression of miR-143 leads to the inhibition of cell growth [8]. The loss of miR-143 expression has been reported in many other cancer types and that restorations of its expression has been shown to abrogate tumorigenesis [9–11]. Several groups, including ours have shown a significant down-regulation of let-7 expression in PC as compared to normal or even histologically normal pancreas tissue adjacent to the tumor, and that the loss of expression was negatively correlated with differentiation, stage of the disease, and patients' survival [12–16].

The let-7 family is known to negatively regulate the expression of Ras, and that the loss of expression of let-7 has been shown to be associated with many cancers including PC [17;18]. Another report suggested that increased expression of Lin-28B which plays an important role in response to radiation in lung cancer cells was indeed mediated through the loss of expression of let-7g and consistent with up-regulation of K-Ras [19]. It has been reported that the high mobility group A protein 2 (HMGA2) whose increased expression is responsible for increased cell growth and contributes to the acquisition of epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT) is in part co-operatively regulated through the activation of Ras signaling pathway in PC [20], and that the ectopic expression of let-7 reduced HMGA2 expression leading to the inhibition of cell proliferation in lung cancer [21]. Other investigators have reported that let-7 could regulate cell cycle, angiogenesis, migration, and all of which could indeed be due to inhibition of K-Ras protein expression [22;23]. To the best of our knowledge, there are no reports showing that a specific let-7 family miRNAs especially let-7i could negatively regulate Ras expression and its GTPase activity that could play important roles in the regulation of tumor aggressiveness in PC.

Emerging evidence suggests that miR-21 is the most frequently up-regulated miRNA in many solid tumors [12;24–27], and there are several miR-21 targets such as PTEN, and PDCD4, which are also down-regulated by Ras in an AP-1 dependent manner in thyroid cell system [26]. In breast cancer, inhibition of miR-21 led to decreased cell proliferation and migration in vitro and tumor growth in vivo [28]. We have also reported earlier showing over-expression of miR-21 in both cell lines and in the plasma of patients diagnosed with PC, which was also linked with the loss of PTEN, Maspin, and TPM1 [12]. These and other evidence led to the recognition that targeted inactivation of Ras signaling is a suitable approach of PC therapy; however, Ras-targeting drug development has been disappointing [1], which further suggest that newer approaches must be developed for targeted inactivation of Ras expression and its GTPase activity with minimal toxicity. To that end, we have synthesized a novel analog of curcumin named CDF which has been shown to have potent anti-tumor activity mediated by multiple mechanisms including deregulation of miRNAs.

In the present study, we investigated the mechanistic role of several miRNAs especially let-7 family, miR-143 and miR-21 in the aggressiveness of PC, and further tested whether these miRNAs mediate their biological activity through the regulation of Ras expression and its activity. During the search for putative miRNA as inhibitors of K-Ras, miRNA target prediction algorithms was utilized, and we found that miR-143 possess a match for base pairing with the 3'-UTR of K-Ras with the context score percentile of 93 (TargetScanHuman 5.2). Although, miRNA target prediction for let-7i was not available by TargetScanHuman, numerous articles have reported let-7 miRNA binding to 3'-UTR of K-Ras [29–33]. Thus, we investigated the putative role of miR-143 and let-7i and their effects on the expression of Ras and its activity by transfecting pre-miR-143 or pre-miR-let-7i in human PC cell lines. We found that re-expression of miRNAs by transfection of cells with pre-miR-143 and let-7i led to decreased cell proliferation and clonogenicity, which was correlated with inhibition of Ras expression and its GTPase activity both in vitro as well as in a xenograft mouse model in vivo. Moreover, instead of transfection, our novel agent, CDF was able to re-express let-7 and miR-143, and down-regulated miR-21 leading to inhibition of cell growth in vitro and tumor growth in vivo, which was consistent with attenuation of Ras expression and its activity. Based on these results, we conclude the importance of specific miRNAs that could serve as key regulators of Ras activity, which could be attenuated by CDF, and thus CDF could be a novel agent for the treatment of human PC.

sec.title: 2. Materials and Methods
2.1. Cells Culture, Drugs and Reagents
sec.p: Human pancreatic cancer cell lines COLO-357, MIAPaCa-2, and BxPC-3 were chosen for this study based on their expression level of Ras and its GTPase activity. The cell lines have been tested and authenticated using the core facility-Applied Genomics Technology Center at Wayne State University on March 13, 2009. The method used for testing was short tandem repeat (STR) profiling using the PowerPlex® 16 System from Promega (Madison, WI). These cells were stored in multiple vials in liquid nitrogen for our use. CDF was synthesized as described in our earlier publications [34;35].

sec.title: 2.2. Ras GTPase Activity
sec.p: Total Ras GTPase activity was measured in both cell lines and in tumor remnants, lysed with beads coated with Raf1-RBD (Millipore, Billerica, Massachusetts). Briefly cells/tumor remnants were lysed in lysis buffer provided by the manufacturer and the total protein was quantitated. 200 μg of protein was used for the assay following the manufacture's protocol. Both positive and negative controls provided in the kit were used in the assay. Western blot was performed and the proteins were transferred to nitrocellulose membrane, and were incubated with anti-Ras provided with the kit for assessing the level of Ras expression.

sec.title: 2.3. Protein extraction and Western blot analysis
sec.p: Total protein was extracted from all three PC cell lines and MIAPaCa-2 tumor remnants from xenograft model untreated and treated with CDF 1 μM or CDF 5mg per mice and subjected to western blot analysis as described previously [36] to evaluate the expression of K-Ras and the data was adjusted against loading control using β-actin expression.

sec.title: 2.4. Anti-sense miR-21 oligonucleotide Transfection
sec.p: COLO-357, BxPC-3 and MIAPaCa-2 cells were plated in 6 well plates and incubated overnight. Cells were transfected with anti-sense miR-21 oligonucleotide (ASO) or control miRNA using ExGen 500 (Fermentas, Hanover, MD) following the manufacture's protocol. Cells were treated with 1 μM of CDF for 48 h and tested the transfection effects of Ras expression by western blot and assayed for Ras GTPase activity.

sec.title: 2.5. TaqMan miRNA Real-Time Reverse Transcriptase-Polymerase Chain Reaction (RT-PCR)
sec.p: To determine the basal level of miRNAs (let-7a, let-7b, let-7c, let-7d, let-7e, let-7f, let-7i, miR-21, and miR-143) in all three PC cell lines, and in tumor samples from xenograft mouse model, we used TaqMan MicroRNA Assay kit (Applied Biosystems) following manufacturer's protocol. Cells were treated with CDF (0.5–1 μM) for 48h and used for the study. Total RNA was extracted and 10 ng from each sample were reverse transcribed as described earlier [12]. Real-time PCR reactions were then carried out in a total volume of 10 μl reaction mixture as described earlier [12]. All reactions, including controls were performed in triplicate using StepOnePlus Real-Time PCR (Applied Biosystems). Relative expression of miRNAs was analyzed using Ct method and was normalized by RNU48 expression.

sec.title: 2.6. Animal Experiments
sec.p: Female CB17 SCID mice (about 4 wks old) were purchased from Taconic Farms (Germantown, NY) and fed Lab Diet 5021 (Purina Mills, Inc., Richmond, IN). MIAPaCa-2 cells-derived pancreatospheres (5000 spheres) were injected subcutaneously in SCID mice bilaterally. Mice were randomized into two treatment groups (n = 7 per group): (1) untreated control; (2) CDF (5 mg/mice/day), intragastric once daily for 12 days. Tumor measurements were performed at multiple time points during the course of treatment. Tumor weights were calculated as described earlier [37]. All the animals were euthanized when the tumor weight in the control group reached approximately 2,000 mg, and the tumor remnants were used for both western blot analysis and qRT-PCR

sec.title: 2.7. Transfection of miRNA precursor miR-143 and let-7i
sec.p: About 200,000 cells/well of COLO-357 or MIAPaCa-2 were seeded in six-well plates and transfected with pre-miR-143, or pre-let-7i and control miRNA (Ambion, Austin, TX) at a final concentration of 20 nM using DharmaFECT transfection reagent (Dharmacon), following the manufacturer's protocol, and as described previously [38]. After 48–72h of transfection, the transfected cells were measured for cell growth by 3-(4,5-dimetylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay, and clonogenic assay was done. The cells were also harvested separately for total RNA and protein extraction and the relative levels of miRNAs, and proteins were measured as described above.

sec.title: 2.8. Cell Viability Assay of transfected cells
sec.p: Standard MTT assay was performed after transfection of cells for 72 h, and experiments were repeated three times. The color intensity was measured by TECAN's microplate fluorometer (TECAN, Research Triangle Park, NC) at 595 nm.

sec.title: 2.9. Clonogenic assay of transfected cells
sec.p: Transfected cells were trypsinized and 1000 viable cells were plated in 100 mm petri dishes. The cells were then incubated for about 10–12 days at 37°C in a 5% CO2 /5% O2 /90% N2 incubator. Colonies were stained with 2% crystal violet and quantitated.

sec.title: 2.10. Transfection of miRNA precursor let-7 i in vivo
sec.p: Effect of pre-let-7i was studied by transfection of expression plasmids with pre-let-7i (Origene Technologies) in MIAPaCa-2 cells prior to injection in the mice. Empty plasmid was transfected as vector control in MIAPaCa-2 cells. Stably transfected cells were selected by adding 800 μg/ml G418 (Sigma, St Louis, MO) in the normal media. About one million MIAPaCa-2 cells (vector control and let-7i –transfected) were injected subcutaneously bilaterally in female ICR-SCID mice. Tumor measurements were performed at multiple time points between parental and pre-let-7i transfected group for four weeks with 5 animals in each group. Tumor weights were calculated. Animals were euthanized at the end of 4 weeks and RNA and protein was extracted from the tumor tissue of both groups to measure the let-7i miRNA expression and Ras GTPase activity levels.

sec.title: 2.11. Statistical Methods
sec.p: Comparisons of treatment outcome were tested for statistical difference by the paired t-test. Statistical significance was assumed at a p value of < 0.05.

sec.title: 3. Results
3.1. Ras expression and its GTPase activity in PC cells was associated with deregulated expression of miRNAs
sec.p: We examined the basal level of Ras expression and its GTPase activity in three human PC cell lines. Ras expression and activity was highly elevated in MIAPaCa-2 cells (mutant KRas), and moderately elevated in COLO-357 cells (mutant K-Ras) compared to wild type K-Ras BxPC-3 cells (Figure 1A). Similarly, several miRNAs were differentially expressed in all three cell lines tested of which let-7 family (let-7a, let-7b, let-7c, let-7d, let-7e, let-7f, let-7i), and miR-143 was significantly lower in MIAPaCa-2 cells compared to COLO-357 and BxPC-3 cells (Figure 1B, 1C). The loss of expression of let-7 family especially let-7i and miR-143 was correlated with the level of Ras expression and its GTPase activity. In contrast, the expression of miR-21 was significantly higher in MIAPaCa-2 cells compared to COLO-357 and BxPC-3 cells (Figure 1C).

sec.title: 3.2. Re-expression of let-7 family, and miR-143 was achieved by CDF treatment
sec.p: We determined the expression levels of let-7 family (let-7a, let-7e, let-7f, let-7i), and miR-143 after treatment of PC cells with 0.5 μM-1 μM of CDF for 48h in all three cell lines (COLO-357, MIAPaCa-2, and BxPC-3 cells). The expression level was determined by real time RT-PCR. We found a significant up-regulation in the expression of let-7i in all three cell lines treated with CDF in a dose dependent manner (Figure 2). Since the basal level of miR-143 was undetectable in MIAPaCa-2 cells, we chose MIAPaCa-2 pancreatospheres tumor derived cells and treated with CDF to see the effect on treatment. We observed a significant up-regulation of miR-143 in pancreatospheres treated with CDF (Figure 2). To further validate whether miR-21 indeed could target the Ras expression and its GTPase activity, we investigated the effect of transfection of miR-21 anti-sense oligonucleotide in all three cell lines and further treated with CDF as presented below.

sec.title: 3.3. Transfection of anti-sense miR-21 in PC cells reduced Ras expression and its GTPase activity
sec.p: COLO-357, BxPC-3, and MIAPaCa-2 cells were used to evaluate the effect of miR-21 anti-sense oligonucleotide transfection on the expression of Ras and its GTPase activity. The cells were transfected with anti-sense miR-21 for 24h followed by CDF treatment for 48h. Both positive and negative controls were included in the assay. The expression of Ras and its GTPase activity was reduced in anti-miR-21 transfection in all three cell lines; however, the effect was much more pronounced in MIAPaCa-2 cells (Figure 3C). These findings suggest that K-Ras oncogene is a target of miR-21, and our synthetic agent CDF was capable of further down-regulation in the expression of Ras and its GTPase activity. In order to recapitulate many important aspects of the human pancreatic tumor, a xenograft animal model was chosen for in vivo studies.

sec.title: 3.4. CDF inhibited pancreatospheres-induced tumor growth and inhibited the expression of Ras and its GTPase activity resulting from the re-expression of miRNAs in tumor remnants in vivo
sec.p: In our previous reports, we have documented the reduction of tumor weight by CDF in MIAPaCa-2 cells induced tumors in a mouse model [39]. In this study, we examined the effects of CDF on tumors derived from pancreatospheres of MIAPaCa-2 cells implanted in mice. CDF treatment group showed significant inhibition in tumor growth compared to untreated controls (Figure 4A). The mice did not show any weight loss during the treatment period, suggesting that this treatment had no major adverse effects on animals. We further tested the effects of treatment on Ras expression and its GTPase activity in tumor remnants. We found that the expression of Ras and its GTPase activity was highly elevated in pancreatospheres derived tumors (Figure. 4B) and as expected, treatment of these tumors in mice with CDF for 12 days showed decreased expression of Ras and its GTPase activity (Figure 4B).

Moreover, the expression level of let-7a, let-7e, let-7f, and let-7i, miRNAs were significantly up-regulated in CDF treated groups (Figure 4C). The pancreatospheres induced tumor and also tumor-derived cells from pancreatospheres showed significant up-regulation of miR-143 when treated with CDF (Figure 4D). In order to investigate the functional roles of miR-143 and let-7i in PC, we examined their effects on cell growth, colony formation, the levels of Ras expression and its GTPase activity, and the expression of miRNAs using COLO-357 and MIAPaCa-2 cells transfected with miR-143, and let-7i precursor as presented below.

sec.title: 3.5. Ras expression and its GTPase activity are regulated by miR-143 and affecting cell proliferation, and colony formation of COLO-357 cells
sec.p: Ras oncogene is known to regulate cell proliferation and transformation [40]. In order to test whether miR-143 expression could regulate Ras expression and its activity, we over-expressed miR-143 with pre-miR-143 in COLO-357 cells which expresses relatively low basal levels of miR-143 compared to BxPC-3 cells. COLO-357 cells were seeded in both 96 and 6 well plates and were transfected with pre-miR-143 precursor for 48h followed by treatment with CDF. Ectopic expression of miR-143 with miR-143 precursor led to increased expression of miR-143 compared to control as tested by qRT-PCR (Figure 5A), which was expected. Interestingly, the re-expression of miR-143 led to decreased expression of Ras and its GTPase activity (Figure 5B).

Moreover, over-expression of miR-143 significantly inhibited cell viability (Figure 5C), and clonogenicity (Figure 5D), and this was further inhibited after CDF treatment compared to control. Since a gene can be a target of more than one miRNAs, we investigated other K-Ras targeting miRNAs that can serve as a mechanism for regulating the expression of Ras and its GTPase activity for which we chose let-7i because its expression was completely lost in MIAPaCa-2 cells.

sec.title: 3.6. Ras expression and its GTPase activity are regulated by let-7i and affecting cell proliferation and colony formation
sec.p: Since MIAPaCa-2 cells showed relatively lowest levels of let-7i expression compared to COLO-357 and BxPC-3 cells, we chose MIAPaCa-2 cells for assessing the effect of re-expression of let-7i by transfection experiments. Cells were seeded in both 96 and 6 well plates and were transfected with let-7i precursor for 48h followed by CDF treatment. Transfection of cells with let-7i precursor showed increased expression of let-7i as determined by qRT-PCR compared to control (Figure 6A), and these results were correlated with decreased expression of Ras and its GTPase activity (Figure. 6B). Transfection of cells with let-7i precursor led to a significant inhibition of cell viability (Figure 6C), and colony formation (Figure 6D), and these effects were much more pronounced after CDF treatment.

sec.title: 3.7. Stably transfected cells with pre-let-7i showed decreased tumor growth rate, and it was correlated with increased expression of let-7i and decreased expression of Ras and its GTPase activity
sec.p: In order to further confirm our in vitro results suggesting that the loss of let-7i is associated with increased levels of Ras expression and its GTPase activity, we chose MIAPaCa-2 cells for assessing tumor growth after re-expression of let-7i. We generated stable clones of MIAPaCa-2 that over-expressing let-7i which showed reduced levels of Ras and its GTPase activity for in vivo study. We carried out in vivo experiment by injecting stably transfected pre-let-7i cells and also vector-transfected control in MIAPaCa-2 cells implanted subcutaneously in SCID mice which led to a differential tumor growth rate (Figure 7). Interestingly, re-expression of let-7i in the tumor specimens showed increased expression of let-7i (Figure 7B), and the reduced tumor growth was consistent with significant reductions in the expression of Ras and its GTPase activity compared to parental MIAPaCa-2 used as control.

sec.title: 4. Discussion
sec.p: Since K-Ras mutation is frequently observed in human pancreatic tumors and cell lines, it has been the focus for the development of Ras inhibitors for therapy. Although effective clinical responses regarding K-Ras targeted therapy have not been validated yet, the goal of our study was to examine the role of specific miRNAs that may regulate the expression of Ras and its activity and further determine whether CDF, a newly developed analog of curcumin, could be useful for targeted inactivation of Ras expression and its activity in PC cells in vitro and in mouse xenograft tumor model in vivo. In this study, initially we investigated the basal level of expression of Ras and its GTPase activity in a panel of three PC cell lines and also in xenograft mouse model. The results showed highly elevated expression of Ras and its GTPase activity in K-Ras mutant human PC cell lines and also in tumors in animal compared to low levels in the K-Ras wild type PC cells (BxPC-3 cells). Previous studies have shown that pancreatic and lung cancer cell lines exhibit significantly lower level of expression of let-7 family, which was associated with increased expression of K-Ras protein compared to normal fibroblasts, suggesting that let-7 could negatively regulate K-Ras expression and its activity [17;18]. In colorectal cancer cell lines, let-7 was not only involved in cell growth, but also modulated tumor sensitivity to chemotherapeutic drugs [41]. Our findings clearly show, for the first time, that re-expression of let-7 family especially let-7i could regulate the expression of Ras and its GTPase activity resulting in the inhibition of tumor growth.

During our investigation we found that multiple miRNAs could potentially target Ras activity in PC cells and we found loss of expression of miRNA let-7 family (let-7a, let-7b, let-7c, let-d, let-7e, let-7f, and let-7i), miR-21, and miR-143 in MIAPaCa-2 cells showing highest levels of Ras expression and its GTPase activity. We also found that CDF could be useful for the re-expression of the lost miRNAs, which in turn reduced the levels of Ras and its GTPase activity both in PC cells in vitro and in tumor in vivo. Numerous reports have shown that Ras, a well known oncogene has been known to be directly targeted by let-7, miR-143, and miR-21. Its over-activity, either by mutation or upstream signaling, often plays important roles in transformation and progression of cancer stem like cells in various cancers, including pancreatic cancer. The deregulation of miRNAs could be associated either with chemo-resistance or chemo-sensitivity. CDF has the capacity of sensitizing cancer cells because of better cellular uptake and retention by cancer cells. It has also been proven to markedly diminish the number of stem cells in vitro and in vivo. Although the exact mechanism by which CDF leads to alterations in the expression of let-7i, miR-143 and miR-21 is not yet known, our data show for the first time that CDF could re-express the lost miRNAs and down-regulate the miRNA that is over-expressed in pancreatic cancer. CDF-mediated alteration in the expression of miRNAs leads to the down-regulation in the expression and the GTPase activity of Ras, which is a novel finding. Unlike loss of expression, we found increased expression of miR-21 in MIAPaCa-2 cells relative to other cell lines and that CDF was able to down-regulate miR-21 expression which was correlated with decreased levels of Ras expression and its GTPase activity. These results are consistent with our previous findings showing that over-expression of miR-21 is associated with tumor aggressiveness [12] and that CDF could function as a novel anti-tumor agent by targeting miR-21 expression [39] and thereby reducing the expression of Ras and its activity.

Previous reports have used transgenic mouse models for targeting different organs for the development of tumors, and have shown that the activation of K-Ras could function as an oncogenic signaling for multiple tumor system including PC [3;42]. Expression of a mutant K-Ras in cancer cells indicates a crucial role in the development of malignant phenotype, and has a defined role in oncogenesis, in regulating cell growth, differentiation and early stages of tumorigenesis in mouse model [43]. We inves tigated the tumor inducing activity of K-Ras mutant MIAPaCa-2 cells derived pancreatospheres in mice, which show cancer stem-like cells (CSC) characteristics. Consistent with the above findings from previous investigators, our in vivo results also showed significant increase in tumor formation induced by pancreatospheres derived from MIAPaCa-2 cells in mice. The increase in tumor growth was reduced by CDF, which could indeed be due to attenuation of Ras expression and its activity mediated by re-expression of let-7 family of miRNAs (let-7a, let-7e, let-7f, let-7i family), and miR-143. The inhibition of tumor growth by CDF was consistent with our previous findings [39].

Among the several let-7 family miRNAs tested both in vitro and in vivo,let-7i showed significantly lower expression in both MIAPaCa-2 cells and pancreatospheres derived tumors. Hence, we tested whether the re-expression of let-7i using pre-let-7i transfection of MIAPaCa-2 cells alone or in combination with CDF treatment could optimally suppress cell growth, colony formation, and whether this effect could be associated with inactivation of Ras expression and its activity. Similarly, in light of the potential role of miR-143 in the regulation of Ras expression and its activity, we also investigated the consequence on the re-expression of miR-143 by transfecting pre-miR-143 in COLO-357 cells, and further determined its direct impact on Ras expression and its activity. Interestingly, we found suppressed cell growth, colony formation, and Ras activity in both MIAPaCa-2 and COLO-357 cells transfected with either let-7i or miR-143 precursor, and that this effect was much more pronounced after CDF treatment.

To further support our in vitro findings, an in vivo tumor model was used to assess the tumor growth capacity of stably transfected MIAPaCa-2 cells with pre-let-7i miRNA. Inhibition of tumor growth in the xenograft mouse model suggests that let-7i re-expression would be a novel strategy for the treatment of PC, which is mediated by inactivation of Ras expression and its activity. Our data provided mechanistic evidence for the first time showing that re-expression of let-7i will regulate the expression of Ras and its GTPase activity, which could be accomplished either by re-expression by transfection or treatment of cells with CDF. In summary, elevated levels of Ras expression and its GTPase activity plays a crucial role in the development and progression of PC, which is regulated by multiple miRNAs specifically let-7i, miR-143, and miR-21 and these processes could be attenuated by CDF. Therefore, we suggest that our novel agent, CDF, could serve as an innovative approach for the treatment of patients diagnosed with PC.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: This work was funded by grants from the National Cancer Institute, NIH 5R01CA131151, 3R01CA131151-02S109, and 1R01CA132794 awarded to FHS. We also thank Puschelberg and Guido foundations for their generous contribution.

fn.p: This is a PDF file of an unedited manuscript that has been accepted for publication. As a service to our customers we are providing this early version of the manuscript. The manuscript will undergo copyediting, typesetting, and review of the resulting proof before it is published in its final citable form. Please note that during the production process errors may be discovered which could affect the content, and all legal disclaimers that apply to the journal pertain.
Conflicts of interest: All the authors declare no competing conflict of interest.

let-7 MicroRNA transfer in pancreatic cancer-derived cells inhibits in vitro cell proliferation but fails to alter tumor progression
Profiling of 95 microRNAs in pancreatic cancer cell lines and surgical specimens by real-time PCR analysis
RAS is regulated by the let-7 microRNA family

LIN28B confers radio-resistance through the posttranscriptional control of KRAS
HMGA2 maintains oncogenic RAS-induced epithelial-mesenchymal transition in human pancreatic cancer cells
The tumor suppressor microRNA let-7 represses the HMGA2 oncogene
The let-7 microRNA represses cell proliferation pathways in human cells
Slowing down the Ras lane: miRNAs as tumor suppressors?

A SNP in a let-7 microRNA complementary site in the KRAS 3' untranslated region increases non-small cell lung cancer risk
New difluoro Knoevenagel condensates of curcumin, their Schiff bases and copper complexes as proteasome inhibitors and apoptosis inducers in cancer cells
Fluorocurcumins as Cyclooxygenase-2 Inhibitor: Molecular Docking, Pharmacokinetics and Tissue Distribution in Mice
Simultaneous targeting of the epidermal growth factor receptor and cyclooxygenase-2 pathways for pancreatic cancer therapy
Concurrent inhibition of NF-kappaB, cyclooxygenase-2, and epidermal growth factor receptor leads to greater anti-tumor activity in pancreatic cancer
Over-expression of FoxM1 leads to epithelial-mesenchymal transition and cancer stem cell phenotype in pancreatic cancer cells
Anti-tumor activity of a novel compound-CDF is mediated by regulating miR-21, miR-200, and PTEN in pancreatic cancer
The miR-18a* microRNA functions as a potential tumor suppressor by targeting on K-Ras
Non-coding MicroRNAs hsa-let-7g and hsa-miR-181b are Associated with Chemoresponse to S-1 in Colon Cancer
Analysis of lung tumor initiation and progression using conditional expression of oncogenic K-ras
Inducible activation of oncogenic K-ras results in tumor formation in the oral cavity

caption.p: Ras GTPase activity and basal levels of K-Ras expression in human pancreatic cancer (PC) cell lines COLO-357, BxPC-3 and MIAPaCa-2 cells. Ras expression and its GTPase activity were significantly higher in MIAPaCa-2 cells followed by COLO-357 and BxPC-3 (A). Relative expressions of let-7a, let-7b, let-7c, let-7d, let-7e, let-7f, let-7i (B), miR-21 and miR-143 (C) as assessed by qRT-PCR in COLO-357, BxPC-3 and MIAPaCa-2 cells. P values represent comparison between BxPC-3 and MIAPaCa-2 cells as calculated by the paired t test.

Comparative level of expression of let-7a, let-7e, let-7f, let-7i, and miR-143 in untreated and treated cells with CDF (0.5– 1 μM) for 48h in COLO-357 (A), BxPC-3 (B) and MIAPaCa-2 cells (C). There was a significant up-regulation of let-7i, and miR-143 expression with CDF treatment. P values represent comparison between untreated and CDF 1 μM treated cells as calculated by the paired t test.

Ras GTPase activity and the levels of K-Ras expression in human PC cell lines after miR-21 anti-sense transfection; COLO-357 (A), BxPC-3 (B) and MIAPaCa-2 cells (C) by western blot analysis. A significant inhibition of Ras GTPase activity and its expression was observed in MIAPaCa-2 and COLO-357 cells transfected with anti-sense miR-21. No such effect was observed in control miRNA transfected cells.

CDF exhibited anti-tumor activity in MIAPaCa-2 pancreatospheres induced tumors in a xenograft mouse model (A), which was consistent with inhibition of Ras expression and its GTPase activity (B), and re-expression of let-7 family (C) and miR-143 expression (D) in tumor remnants. The arrow indicates starting day of the treatment. P values were calculated by the paired t test.

Re-expression of miR-143 by pre-miR-143 precursor or CDF treatment in COLO-357 cells showed increased expression of miR-143 as assessed by qRT-PCR (A), and corresponding decrease expression of Ras and its GTPase activity as assessed by western blot (B) compared to control cells. The above treatment also led to reduced cell viability as assessed by MTT assay (C) and clonogenicity as assessed by colony formation assay (D).

Re-expression of let-7i by pre-let-7i precursor transfection or treatment of MIAPaCa-2 cells by CDF led to increased expression of let-7i as assessed by qRT-PCR (A), and showed decreased levels of Ras expression and its GTPase activity (B) compared to control cells. The above treatment also led to reduced cell viability as assessed by MTT assay (C) and clonogenicity as determined by colony formation assay (D).

Over-expression of let-7i using stably transfected pre-let-7i in MIAPaCa-2 cells in vivo showed decreased tumor growth rate in a subcutaneous tumor xenograft model (A), consistent with increased levels of let-7i expression (B), which was associated with decreased levels of Ras expression and its activity (C) compared to control vector MIAPaCa-2 cells. n=5 mice for each group.
